intro:
If you earn an annual salary, knowing your equivalent hourly rate helps you evaluate whether extra projects are worth your time, compare job offers, calculate overtime value, or understand what you actually earn per hour of effort. A $65,000 annual salary translates to $31.25/hour at standard full-time hours — but what if you routinely work 50-hour weeks? Your effective rate drops to $25/hour.

Our salary to hourly calculator divides your annual salary by total hours worked per year (hours/week × weeks/year) to show your equivalent hourly rate, along with daily, weekly, and monthly breakdowns.

This calculation is especially valuable for freelancers setting client rates, employees deciding whether to take on extra hours, or anyone comparing a salaried offer against a contractor or hourly position.
howItWorks: Enter your annual salary, hours worked per week, and working weeks per year. The calculator divides annual salary by total annual hours for your equivalent hourly rate. It then shows daily (rate × 8), weekly (rate × hours/week), and monthly (annual ÷ 12) breakdowns.
interpretationGuide: Your hourly rate is the true cost of your time per hour. If you work more than 40 hours per week, your effective hourly rate is lower than the standard calculation suggests. Use this to assess overtime value, freelance rates, and whether extra projects are worth the time investment.

faqs.question: What does a $50,000 salary work out to per hour?
faqs.answer: At standard 40 hrs/week × 52 weeks = 2,080 hours: $50,000 ÷ 2,080 = $24.04/hour. Adjust hours per week in the calculator for your actual schedule.

faqs.question: Why do salaried workers often earn less per hour than they think?
faqs.answer: Many salaried workers are exempt from overtime and routinely work 45–55 hours per week without additional pay. A $70,000 salary at 50 hours/week = $26.92/hour, not $33.65/hour (the 40-hour calculation). Always divide by your actual hours worked.

faqs.question: How should I use my hourly rate to set freelance prices?
faqs.answer: As a freelancer, your rate must cover taxes (self-employment tax adds ~15%), benefits you provide yourself, unpaid admin time, and profit. A common rule: multiply your desired employee equivalent hourly rate by 2–2.5× for a fair freelance rate that covers overhead and non-billable time.

faqs.question: What is the effective hourly rate if I work 50 hours per week?
faqs.answer: For a $70,000 salary at 50 hours/week: 50 × 52 = 2,600 total hours. $70,000 ÷ 2,600 = $26.92/hour. The same salary at 40 hours/week would be $33.65/hour — showing how overtime significantly reduces effective pay.

faqs.question: How many hours per year does a full-time worker typically work?
faqs.answer: The US standard is 2,080 hours (40 hrs × 52 weeks). Accounting for average paid time off (10–15 days) and federal holidays (11 days), actual worked hours are typically 1,800–1,900 per year. Use actual hours for the most accurate hourly rate.

faqs.question: Should I include bonus in my annual salary for this calculation?
faqs.answer: For a conservative baseline, use your base salary only. If your bonus is guaranteed or consistent, you can add it to get total annual compensation. For hourly comparisons, most people use base salary since bonuses vary.

faqs.question: How does salary differ from total compensation?
faqs.answer: Base salary is just cash earnings. Total compensation adds the value of health insurance (often $5,000–$15,000/year), 401(k) match, paid time off (2 weeks PTO at $50k salary = $1,923 value), stock options, and other perks. Total compensation often exceeds base salary by 25–40%.

faqs.question: What hourly rate do I need to match a $100,000 salary?
faqs.answer: At standard hours: $100,000 ÷ 2,080 = $48.08/hour. As a freelancer or contractor, you typically need $75–$100/hour to net the equivalent after taxes, benefits, and overhead — which is why contractor rates are often significantly higher than equivalent employee rates.

faqs.question: How do I calculate my overtime rate from my salary?
faqs.answer: For non-exempt salaried workers, the regular rate is (Annual Salary ÷ 52 weeks) ÷ 40 hours. Overtime (over 40 hours/week) is paid at 1.5× this rate. Note: many salaried workers are classified as 'exempt' and do not receive overtime pay regardless of hours worked.

faqs.question: Can I use this to compare a salary job to a contract position?
faqs.answer: Yes. Calculate the hourly equivalent of the salaried position, then compare to the contract hourly rate. Remember the contract rate must cover self-employment taxes (~15%), health insurance, retirement savings, paid time off, and business expenses that an employer would normally cover.
